coredata.dc:title: Online estimation for parameters and state-of-charge of LiMn2O2 batteries with a modified adaptive Kalman filter
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Zheng
Li, Shuangqi
Li, Jianwei
He, Hongwen
coredata.dc:description: The accurate estimation for parameters and state-of-charge (SOC) is very important for the state monitoring and optimization in battery management system. This paper introduces an online estimation method with modified Kalman filter, where two contributions are added comparing related battery-application literature: (1) In order to improve the robustness to systematic noise brought by varying current loads and condition, an adaptive law is introduced to calibrate the noise covariance recursively. (2) To enhance the numerical stability over covariance propagating, a modified square-root procedure is derived by using sequential measurement update and the Potter algorithm. Besides, a test bench with a series-connected battery pack of 10 LiMn2O2 cells is used to evaluate the algorithm. The experimental results indicate both a better precision comparing the offline result, and the maximum error for the overall SOC is less than 2%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
State-of-charge
Online estimation
square root
Adaptive extended Kalman filter
Experiments

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

xocs:doc.xocs:rawtext: ScienceDirect Available online at www.sciencedirect.comAvailable online at w.sciencedirect.com ScienceDirect Energy Procedia 00 (2017) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 1876-6102 Â© 2017 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. Peer-review under responsibility of the Scientific Committee of The 15th International Symposium on District Heating and Cooling. The 15th International Symposium on District Heating and Cooling Assessing the feasibility of using the heat demand-outdoor temperature function for a long-term district heat demand forecast I. AndriÄ‡a,b,c*, A. Pinaa, P. FerrÃ£oa, J. Fournierb., B. LacarriÃ¨rec, O. Le Correc aIN+ Center for Innovation, Technology and Policy Research - Instituto Superior TÃ©cnico, Av. Rovisco Pais 1, 1049-001 Lisbon, Portugal bVeolia Recherche & Innovation, 291 Avenue Dreyfous Daniel, 78520 Limay, France cDÃ©partement SystÃ¨mes Ã‰nergÃ©tiques et Environnement - IMT Atlantique, 4 rue Alfred Kastler, 44300 Nantes, France Abstract District heating networks are commonly addressed in the literature as one of the most effective solutions for decreasing the greenhouse gas emissions from the building sector. These systems require high investments which are returned through the heat sales. Due to the changed climate conditions and building renovation policies, heat demand in the future could decrease, prolonging the investment return period. The main scope of this paper is to assess the feasibility of using the heat demand â€“ outdoor temperature function for heat demand forecast. The district of Alvalade, located in Lisbon (Portugal), was used as a case study. The district is consisted of 665 buildings that vary in both construction period and typology. Three weather scenarios (low, medium, high) and three district renovation scenarios were developed (shallow, intermediate, deep). To estimate the error, obtained heat demand values were compared with results from a dynamic heat demand model, previously developed and validated by the authors. The results showed that when only weather change is considered, the margin of error could be acceptable for some applications (the error in annual demand was lower than 20% for all weather scenarios considered). However, after introducing renovation scenarios, the error value increased up to 59.5% (depending on the weather and renovation scenarios combination considered). The value of slope coefficient increased on average within the range of 3.8% up to 8% per decade, that corresponds to the decrease in the number of heating hours of 22-139h during the heating season (depending on the combination of weather and renovation scenarios considered). On the other hand, function intercept increased for 7.8-12.7% per decade (depending on the coupled scenarios). The values suggested could be used to modify the function parameters for the scenarios considered, and improve the accuracy of heat demand estimations. Â© 2017 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. Peer-review under responsibility of the Scientific Committee of The 15th International Symposium on District Heating and Cooling. Keywords: Heat demand; Forecast; Climate change Energy Procedia 159 ( 9) 497 5 2 1876-6102 Â© 2019 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd. This is an open access article under the CC-BY-NC-ND license (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/) Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the Applied Energy Symposium and Forum, Renewable Energy Integration with Mini/Microgrids, REM 2018. 10.1016/j.egypro.2018.12.034 10.1016/j.egypro.2018.12.034 Â© 2019 The Auth rs. Published by Els vier Ltd. This is an open access article under the CC-BY-NC-ND license (https://creativecommon .org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/) Selection and peer-review under resp sibility of the scientific committee of the Applied Energy Symposium and Forum, Renewable Energy Integration with Mini/Microgrids, REM 2018. 1876-6102 Available online at www.sciencedirect.com ScienceDirect Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia 1876-6102 Copyright Â© 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the Applied Energy Symposium and Forum, Renewable Energy Integration with Mini/Microgrids, REM 2018. Applied Energy Symposium and Forum, Renewable Energy Integration with Mini/Microgrids, REM 2018, 29â€“30 September 2018, Rhodes, Greece Online estimation for parameters and state-of-charge f LiMn2O2 batteries with a modified adaptive Kalman filter Zheng Zhang, Shuangqi Li, Jianwei Li, Hongwen He* National Engineering Laboratory for Electric Vehicles, School of Mechanical Engineering, Beijing Institute of Technology, Beijing 100081, China Abstract The accurate estimation for parameters and state-of-charge (SOC) is very important for the state monitoring and optimization in battery management system. This paper introduces an online estimation method with modified Kalman filter, where two contributions are added comparing related battery-application literature: (1) In order to improve the robustness to systematic noise brought by varying current loads and condition, an adaptive law is introduced to calibrate the noise covariance recursively. (2) To enhance the numerical stability over covariance propagating, a modified square-root procedure is derived by using sequential measurement update and the Potter algorithm. Besides, a test bench with a series-connected battery pack of 10 LiMn2O2 cells is used to evaluate the algorithm. The experimental results indicate both a better precision comparing the offline result, and the maximum error for the overall SOC is less than 2%. Copyright Â© 2018 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. Selection and peer-review under responsibility of the scientific committee of the Applied Energy Symposium and Forum, Renewable Energy Integration with Mini/Microgrids, REM 2018. Keywords: Lithium-ion battery; State-of-charge; Online estimation; square root; Adaptive extended Kalman filter; Experiments; 1. Introduction To address the two urgent tasks nowadays of protecting the environment and achieving energy sustainability, the lithium-ion battery is widely used in many fields due to its advantage of zero-emission, long cycle-life and high energy density. One of the typical applications is the scenery for electric vehicle. Usually hundreds of battery cells are * Corresponding author. Tel.: +86-010-68914842; fax: +86-010-68914842.. E-mail address: hwhebit@bit.edu.cn il l li i i . . . . , , . , , , , . . .. . . 498 Zheng Zhang et al. / Energy Procedia 159 (2019) 497â€“502 2 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 parallely-/serially- connected and grouped into a pack, which relies on a powerful battery management system (BMS) to balance the diversity of heat/energy. Where, some states/parameters, like state of charge/power/health (SOC/P/H), open-circuit voltage (OCV) and equivalent internal resistance, could provide essential information to the charge/discharge controlling and health monitoring. However, itâ€™s hard to measure them accurately considering the strong nonlinearity and time-varying property of battery, especially under varying loads and environments. Thus, it has to propose an online estimation scheme for states and parameters in the practical application. It has been proved that the Ohmic and polarization effects increase at the two-end of the energy expenditure, thus fitting error would be enlarged due to the model parameters variation, and many offline optimizations are time-costing and hard to take loads and environments into consideration [1]. On the other hand, during the Kalman filterâ€™s application, the covariance is better to be estimated online, although some adaptive laws are presented, mostly the robustness is not stable, the reason could be owing to the numerical stability and the degradation caused by the modification. Consequently, an online adaptive estimation for state and parameters with high numerical stability is very essential for the battery application. In this paper, a modified adaptive Kalman filter is proposed and implemented on the online estimation for the OCV, equivalent resistance and the other model parameters of 41V/36.6Ah LiMn2O2 battery module. Several typical test are carried out as load profiles on the battery test bench, by which the algorithm is verified through comparisons between the online and offline error calculation. Besides, the SOC estimation is conducted according to the identified OCV and OCV-SOC characteristic curve, and the error analysis turns out a good precision by experiments and comparisons with the true values of SOC. 2. Modeling for the LiFeMnO4-battery module In Ref [2], seven different battery models are introduced and evaluated on the fitting effect. Here we select Thevenin model due to its remarkable relaxation effect of the lithium-ion battery. Its schematic diagram is shown in Fig.1, and the electrical behavior of the proposed model is expressed by Eq. (1). Fig. 1 The schematic diagram of the Thevenin model Fig. 2 The schematic of the battery test bench 0 1 1 t OCV P L p P L p p p U U U R I U U I R C C = âˆ’ âˆ’ï£± ï£´ ï£² = âˆ’ +ï£´ ï£³ ï€¦ (1) , , 0 , , , 1 , 1 (1 ) s s p p p p t i OCV p i L i T T R C R C p i p i p L i U U U R I U e U R I e âˆ’ âˆ’ âˆ’ âˆ’ = âˆ’ âˆ’ï£± ï£´ ï£² ï£´ = + âˆ’ï£³ (2) Where ð��¼ð��¼ð��¼ð��¼ð��¿ð��¿ð��¿ð��¿ is the load current with a positive value at discharge and a negative value at charge , ð�‘…ð�‘…ð�‘…ð�‘…0 is the batteryâ€™s ohmic resistance, ð�‘…ð�‘…ð�‘…ð�‘…ð�‘�ð�‘�ð�‘�ð�‘�, ð��¶ð��¶ð��¶ð��¶ð�‘�ð�‘�ð�‘�ð�‘� are the equivalent polarization resistance and capacitance respectively. The batteryâ€™s OCV is denoted by ð�‘ˆð�‘ˆð�‘ˆð�‘ˆð�‘œð�‘œð�‘œð�‘œð�‘œð�‘œð�‘œð�‘œð�‘œð�‘œð�‘œð�‘œ , ð�‘ˆð�‘ˆð�‘ˆð�‘ˆð�‘�ð�‘�ð�‘�ð�‘� is the voltage over the capacitance ð��¶ð��¶ð��¶ð��¶ð�‘�ð�‘�ð�‘�ð�‘�, ð�‘ˆð�‘ˆð�‘ˆð�‘ˆð�‘¡ð�‘¡ð�‘¡ð�‘¡ is the terminal voltage. 3. Development for the Modified adaptive Kalman filter 3.1. Conventional extended Kalman filter Considering a linear discrete system with additive noise inputs, the system and measurement equations are depicted as Eq. (3), then the process of Kalman filter are shown in Eq. (5)-(9). The contribution of the EKF is to take the Zheng Zhang et al. / Energy Procedia 159 (2019) 497â€“502 499 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 3 derivatives of the dynamic equations regarding the state, then the propagation matrix, namely, 1kâˆ’Î¦ , 1kâˆ’Î“ and kH , would be derived to realize the time and measurement updating for the state and its covariance. Although this approximation still has fitting error between the discrete points, it does provide a conductive way to a nonlinear system. 1 1 1 1 , ~ (0, ) , ~ (0, ) k k k k k k k k k k k k k k x x u w w Q z H x v v R âˆ’ âˆ’ âˆ’ âˆ’= Î¦ + Î“ +ï£± ï£² = +ï£³ (3) (5) (6) ( , ) , ~ (0, ) ( ) , ~ (0, ) k k k k k k x f x u w w Q z M x v v R = +ï£± ï£² = +ï£³ ï€¦ (7) (4) (8) 1 1 1 1 1 1 1 1 ( , ) ( , ) ( ) = , = , =k k k k kk k k k k k f x u f x u M x H x x x âˆ’ âˆ’ âˆ’ âˆ’ âˆ’ âˆ’ âˆ’ âˆ’ âˆ‚ âˆ‚ âˆ‚ Î¦ Î“ âˆ‚ âˆ‚ âˆ‚ (9) where Î¦ and Î“ are the system and control matrix within the transition equation, H is the observation matrix, x and z are, respectively, the state and measurement vector, their corresponding noise are denoted by kw and kv , which are uncorrelated zero-mean Gaussian white noise, as described in Eq. (3) , kQ is a non-negative definite matrix, and kR is a positive one. The similar symbols in Eq. (4)-(9) have the same meaning, the sub-symbol + and âˆ’ denote the priori and posterior estimation, P is the covariance of the state, and the kalman gain is represented by kK ; 3.2. Square root recursive: Potter measurement update In order to develop a modified algorithm with square root propagation, here we introduce the Potter measurement update in [3], which is as follows,Assume the measurement noise kR is a one-element positive value and the posterior covariance kË†P âˆ’ is able to be expressed as Ë† Ë†Ë† = T k k kP S S âˆ’ âˆ’ âˆ’ , then Eqs. (7) and (9) could be rewritten as, Ë† Ë† = Ë† Ë† T T T k k k k T k k k k k S S H K H S S H R âˆ’ âˆ’ âˆ’ âˆ’ + (10) Ë† Ë† Ë† Ë† Ë† Ë† Ë† Ë†Ë† = =Ë† Ë† Ë† Ë† T T T T T T T T k k k k k k k k k k k k kT T k k k k k k k k k k S S H H S H H SP I S S S I S H S S H R H S S H R âˆ’ âˆ’ âˆ’ âˆ’ + âˆ’ âˆ’ âˆ’ âˆ’ âˆ’ âˆ’ âˆ’ âˆ’ ï£« ï£¶ ï£« ï£¶ âˆ’ âˆ’ï£¬ ï£· ï£¬ ï£·ï£¬ ï£· ï£¬ ï£·+ +ï£­ ï£¸ ï£­ ï£¸ (11) Let Ë†= Tk kS HÏ† âˆ’ , =1/( )T ka RÏ† Ï† + , then ( )Ë† Ë†Ë† = T T k k kP S I a SÏ†Ï† + âˆ’ âˆ’âˆ’ , furthermore, we assume ( )2=T TI a I aÏ†Ï† Î³Ï†Ï†âˆ’ âˆ’ , one can deduce =1/(1 )kaRÎ³ Â± , thus kË†P + can be reformed as ( )( )Ë† Ë†Ë† = T T Tk k kP S I a I a SÎ³Ï†Ï† Î³Ï†Ï†+ âˆ’ âˆ’âˆ’ âˆ’ , considering kË†P+ is positive matrix and have Ë† Ë†Ë† = T k k kP S S + + + , the updating for state covariance can be conducted as Eq. (12). ( )Ë† Ë†= Tk kS I a SÎ³Ï†Ï†+ âˆ’âˆ’ (12) Particularly, in order to expand the applicability when kR doesnâ€™t satisfy the assumption, a sequential method for the measurement updating is introduced if and only if kR is a diagonal matrix, the brief procedure is depicted as follows, 3.3. Adaptive rule Due to the sensitivity to the working loads and environment noise, the model errors are hardly to omit in the dynamic equation, thus followed by a problem that how to estimate the kQ and kR , which maybe time-varying during the iterative process. Many adaptive laws have been investigated and applied to the derivatives of the Kalman filter, here we introduce a covariance matching based process noise scaling algorithm for the online estimation of kQ [4]. Assuming the innovation sequence is ï€¤= kk k kz H xÎµ âˆ’ âˆ’ , and substituting Eq. (2) into it, then taking the variances on both sides, one have ï€¤ ï€¤( )= =k kk k k k k kz H x H x x vÎµ âˆ’ âˆ’âˆ’ âˆ’ + (13) { } { }Ë† Ë†=T T T Tk k k k k k k k k k kE H P H E v v H P H RÎµ Îµ âˆ’ âˆ’+ +ï�€ (14) During the filter process, the innovation covariance { }Tk kE Îµ Îµ is available through using a limited number of innovation samples, as shown in Eq. (14), and it also holds the relationship in Eq. (15) { } 1 0 1 mT T k k k i k i i E d d m Îµ Îµ âˆ’ âˆ’ âˆ’ = = âˆ‘ (15) 1 0 1 m T T k i k i k k k k i d d H P H R m âˆ’ âˆ’ âˆ’ âˆ’ = = +âˆ‘ ï€¥ (16) ï‚µ ( ) ï‚µk kk kP I K H P + âˆ’ = âˆ’ ï‚µ ï‚µ 11 1 1 T k kk k kP P Q âˆ’ + âˆ’âˆ’ âˆ’ âˆ’= Î¦ Î¦ + ï€¤ ï€¤ 11 1 1k kk k kx x u âˆ’ + âˆ’âˆ’ âˆ’ âˆ’= Î¦ +Î“ ï‚µ ï‚µ( ) 1T Tk kk k k k kK P H H P H R âˆ’âˆ’ âˆ’= + ï€¤ ï€¤ ï€¤( )k k kk k kx x K z H x+ âˆ’ âˆ’= + âˆ’ 500 Zheng Zhang et al. / Energy Procedia 159 (2019) 497â€“502 4 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 Where, kP âˆ’ ï€¥ is the estimated value based on the innovation sequence. For the kQ estimation, the basic idea is find a way to regulate the process noise according to the differences between the kP âˆ’ ï€¥ and the model-predicted one, kË†P âˆ’ . Hence, we assume kR is perfectly known for adaption, and a scaling factor is introduce as follows, ï‚µ( ) ï‚µ( ) { } ï‚µ{ } ï‚µ{ } 1 11 1 1 0 11 1 1 1 { }{ } Ë†{ } Ë†{ } m TT T T k i k i kkk k k k k k k k ik T T T T k k k kk k k k k k k k k trace d d Rtrace H P Q H trace H P Htrace Q m trace Q trace H P Q H trace H P H trace H P H Î± âˆ’ + âˆ’ âˆ’ âˆ’âˆ’âˆ’ âˆ’ âˆ’ = + âˆ’ âˆ’ âˆ’âˆ’ âˆ’ âˆ’ ï£± ï£¼ âˆ’ï£² ï£½Î¦ Î¦ + ï£³ ï£¾= â‰ˆ = = Î¦ Î¦ + âˆ‘ï€¥ ï€¥ ï€¥ (17) Thus the adaptive rule can be intuitively defined as, Ë† k kQ QÎ±=ï€¥ (18) Where the Î± is a value either larger than or smaller than 1, so this coefficients provide a considerably full varying range for the updating on kQ , particularly, the square root here is used to smooth the evolution process. 3.4. Implementation of the modified Kalman filter on the battery model Here the implementation of the modified Kalman filter on the Thevenin model is presented. For the online estimation of OCV, equivalent resistance and the polarization parameters, the system states and control inputs are defined as follows, 0 1 1[ , , , , ] T t ocvx U U R R Ï„= Lu I= (19) 1 1 , , 1 , 1 , 1 , , 1 0, 0, 1 , , 1 1 , (1 ): , , , : s s i i T T p i p i p i L i OCV i OCV i i i p i p i i i k OCV i U e U R I ef U U R R R R M Z U Ï„ Ï„ Ï„ Ï„ âˆ’ âˆ’ âˆ’ âˆ’ âˆ’ âˆ’ âˆ’ âˆ’ âˆ’ âˆ’ âˆ’ ï£±ï£´ = + âˆ’ï£² ï£´ = = = =ï£³ = (20) Itâ€™s acknowledged that the batteryâ€™s dynamic response highly relies on the state parameters, like SOC, working environment, especially the temperature, and the usage history. Hence the evolution of the model parameters can be described by the function of SOC T hï¼Œ ï¼Œ , for example, we take the derivative of the ocvU with respect to time t : ( , , ) OCV OCV OCVOCV OCV U U USOC T hU U SOC T h SOC t T t h t âˆ‚ âˆ‚ âˆ‚âˆ‚ âˆ‚ âˆ‚ = = + + âˆ‚ âˆ‚ âˆ‚ âˆ‚ âˆ‚ âˆ‚ ï€¦ ï€¦ (21) â‡’ 0OCVU =ï€¦ (22) With the following assumptions: The / 0SOC tâˆ‚ âˆ‚ â‰ˆ holds for that small energy is consumed or charged comparing to the totally usable capacity; The / 0T tâˆ‚ âˆ‚ â‰ˆ holds for that the temperature should be slow-varying with a proper cooling system; The / 0h tâˆ‚ âˆ‚ â‰ˆ definitely holds since the lifetime for battery is considerably long, hence, Eq. (21) could be simplified as (22), and the considerations for the other parameters like 0R , pR , and Ï„ , are the same, here time constant = P PR CÏ„ . Therefore, combining with the differentia equation (2), the differential equations for the filter are organized as Eq. (20). The flowchart of the application can be inferred from Fig. 3, Fig.3 the flowchart of the modified adaptive EKF with Potter algorithm Zheng Zhang et al. / Energy Procedia 159 (2019) 497â€“502 501 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 5 Where 0 ,Q RS Sï¼Œ are, respectively, the upper triangular matrix of Q and R after the Cholesky decomposition. As the measurement used in Kalman filter could be pre-estimated, the adaptive law is only applied to estimation of the QS . 4. Online estimation for the parameters and State-of-charge under experimental validation 4.1. test bench and profile The schematic of the test bench is presented in Fig.2, which includes an Arbin battery test system BT2000, MITS pro soft for programming, a thermal chamber and a host computer. he measurements consist of voltage, temperature, Amp-hours (Ah) and Watt-hours (Wh) etc., which are transmitted to the host computer through TCP/IP ports. The errors of the hall current and voltage sensors are less than 0.1%. Besides, 10 LiMn2O4 battery cells are serially connected into a battery pack, and are kept in the thermal chamber with the temperature controlled within 30 5 CÎ¿Â± . The detailed information about the aforementioned test can be inferred in Ref. [5]. Fig.4 The OCV-SOC table and test profile: (a) the OCV vs. SOC profiles; (b) the current vs. time profiles under one cycle of HPPC test; (c) the current vs. time profiles under one cycle of UDDS From Fig.4(a), due to the equilibrium potential during the forward and reverse reaction, the hysteresis could be observed through the difference of the OCV regarding the same SOC, thus there will exist two SOC values if the OCV-based SOC estimation is applied. However, considering the OCV difference is within 0.3, it is no more than 1% after taking the ratio of the difference to nominal voltage, thus we take the average as the OCV-SOC inference table. 4.2. the estimation result for parameters and SOC Fig.5 Online estimation results: (a) polarization voltage pU ; (b) OCV; (c) R ; (d) PR ; (e)Time constant Ï„ ; (f) the relative error profiles of terminal voltage with estimated parameters. 502 Zheng Zhang et al. / Energy Procedia 159 (2019) 497â€“502 6 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 Fig. 6 the verification of the modified algorithm under UDDS: (a) the estimation for R ; (b) the relative error profiles of terminal voltage with estimated parameters; (c) the estimation for SOC and its error profiles Through a sequence of HPPC profiles, the online estimation is conducted for the modelâ€™s parameters and states, the results are presented in Fig.5. In order to evaluate the performance of the proposed algorithm, we adopt genetic algorithm to make an offline optimization as comparisons, some detailed information could be inferred in Ref. [6]. For the verification, 14 UDDS cycles are employed to test the online estimation approach, as shown in Fig.6. With more dynamic process, the equivalent resistance R is selected in (a) to show the effect of the parameters estimation, and the model fitting effect is depicted by the relative error of terminal voltage. Besides, we take the conventional EKF into consideration for a better comparison, basing on the previous OCV-SOC table, the estimations for SOC and their errors are also presented as shown in (c). Overall, the modified AEKF algorithm has a steadier and smaller error in estimation. Particularly, the error of SOC estimated by the modified algorithm is no more than 0.01, and a phenomenon can be observed that the error increases during the mid-term of the SOC expenditure, which are 0.3~0.7, it's owing to the slow-changing part in the SOC-OCV table. Thus, the proposed algorithm can satisfy the SOC estimation quite well. 5. Conclusions In this paper, a modified adaptive EKF algorithm is developed and employed for the online parameters and states estimation of the LiMn2O2 battery module. With the prior knowledge of the measurement sensor noise, the Potter algorithm enables the square root format for the information propagation, and it significantly improves the effect of the estimation combing with the introduced adaptive law. The implementation with theses modification are elaborated. And the OCV/HPPC/BJDC tests are organized for experimental testing and verification, the results indicate the proposed algorithm is more accurate and preferable in the online application comparing to the offline and conventional EKF, and the estimation error for SOC can guaranteed within 2% under UDDS test. References [1] Liu, Z. and H. He, Sensor fault detection and isolation for a lithium-ion battery pack in electric vehicles using adaptive extended Kalman filter â˜†. Applied Energy, 2016. [2] Hu, X., S. Li, and H. Peng, A comparative study of equivalent circuit models for Li-ion batteries. Journal of Power Sources, 2012. 198(198): p. 359-367. [3] Dan, S., Optimal State Estimation: Kalman, Hâˆž, and Nonlinear Approaches. 2006: Wiley-Interscience. [4] Ding, W., et al., Improving Adaptive Kalman Estimation in GPS/INS Integration. Journal of Navigation, 2007. 60(3): p. 517-529. [5] Xiong, R., et al., Model-based state of charge and peak power capability joint estimation of lithium-ion battery in plug-in hybrid electric vehicles. Journal of Power Sources, 2013. 229(9): p. 159-169. [6] He, H., R. Xiong, and H. Guo, Online estimation of model parameters and state-of-charge of LiFePO 4 batteries in electric vehicles. Applied Energy, 2012. 89(1): p. 413-420. cycle of UDDS From Fig.4(a), due to the equilibrium potential during the forward and reverse reaction, the hysteresis could be observed through the difference of the OCV regarding the same SOC, thus there will exist two SOC values if the OCV-based SOC estimation is applied. However, considering the OCV difference is within 0.3, it is no more than 1% after taking the ratio of the difference to nominal voltage, thus we take the average as the OCV-SOC inference table. 4.2. the estimation result for parameters and SOC Fig.5 Online estimation results: (a) polarization voltage pU ; (b) OCV; (c) R ; (d) PR ; (e)Time constant Ï„ ; (f) the relative error profiles of terminal voltage with estimated parameters. 502 Zheng Zhang et al. / Energy Procedia 159 (2019) 497â€“502 6 Author name / Energy Procedia 00 (2018) 000â€“000 Fig. 6 the verification of the modified algorithm under UDDS: (a) the estimation for R ; (b) the relative error profiles of terminal voltage with estimated parameters; (c) the estimation for SOC and its error profiles Through a sequence of HPPC profiles, the online estimation is conducted for the modelâ€™s parameters and states, the results are presented in Fig.5. In order to evaluate the performance of the proposed algorithm, we adopt genetic algorithm to make an offline optimization as comparisons, some detailed information could be inferred in Ref. [6

head.ce:title: Online estimation for parameters and state-of-charge of LiMn2O2 batteries with a modified adaptive Kalman filter

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Engineering Laboratory for Electric Vehicles, School of Mechanical Engineering, Beijing Institute of Technology, Beijing 100081, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Engineering Laboratory for Electric Vehicles

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The accurate estimation for parameters and state-of-charge (SOC) is very important for the state monitoring and optimization in battery management system. This paper introduces an online estimation method with modified Kalman filter, where two contributions are added comparing related battery-application literature: (1) In order to improve the robustness to systematic noise brought by varying current loads and condition, an adaptive law is introduced to calibrate the noise covariance recursively. (2) To enhance the numerical stability over covariance propagating, a modified square-root procedure is derived by using sequential measurement update and the Potter algorithm. Besides, a test bench with a series-connected battery pack of 10 LiMn2O2 cells is used to evaluate the algorithm. The experimental results indicate both a better precision comparing the offline result, and the maximum error for the overall SOC is less than 2%.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sensor fault detection and isolation for a lithium-ion battery pack in electric vehicles using adaptive extended Kalman filter ☆.
A comparative study of equivalent circuit models for Li-ion batteries
Optimal State Estimation: Kalman, H∞, and Nonlinear Approaches
Improving Adaptive Kalman Estimation in GPS/INS Integration
Model-based state of charge and peak power capability joint estimation of lithium-ion battery in plug-in hybrid electric vehicles
